content: Adult dogs typically need 30 to 120 minutes of exercise per day depending on breed. High-energy breeds like Golden Retrievers, Border Collies, and Huskies often need 60 to 90 minutes of vigorous activity. Under-exercised dogs can develop destructive behaviors.

Most adult dogs do best with two meals per day, spaced about 8 to 12 hours apart. Puppies under 6 months need three to four smaller meals. Avoid strenuous exercise within one hour of feeding to reduce risk of bloat.
Most dogs only need a bath every 4 to 8 weeks. Bathing too often strips natural oils and can cause dry skin. Brushing 2 to 3 times per week helps control shedding and distributes skin oils.

Cats are natural grazers and typically do best with two measured meals per day. Free-feeding dry food can lead to obesity. Wet food supports hydration, which is critical for urinary and kidney health.
Indoor cats need daily mental stimulation. Short play sessions of 10 to 15 minutes, twice a day, using wand toys mimic hunting behavior and reduce stress, anxiety, and destructive scratching.
Long-haired cats like Persians need daily brushing to prevent matting. Short-haired cats benefit from brushing once or twice a week. Regular grooming also reduces hairballs.

Puppies under one year need more frequent meals, short but frequent play sessions, and consistent potty breaks every 2 to 3 hours. Socialization between 8 and 16 weeks is critical for long-term behavior.

Senior dogs and cats (age 7 and older) benefit from shorter, gentler exercise sessions and more frequent vet checkups. Joint-friendly activities like slow walks or gentle play are better than high-impact exercise.
Walk dogs in the early morning or evening when temperatures are cooler. Avoid midday walks in hot weather, especially on asphalt which can burn paw pads. Always provide fresh water.

Short-haired dogs and small breeds get cold quickly. Limit outdoor time in freezing weather, and consider a coat for breeds like greyhounds or chihuahuas. Wipe paws after walks to remove salt and de-icer.
Dental disease affects most pets by age 3. Brush your pet's teeth 2 to 3 times per week with pet-safe toothpaste. Dental chews and annual cleanings help prevent periodontal disease.
Give medications at the same time each day for consistency. Many pet medications work best when given with food to reduce stomach upset. Never skip doses without consulting a vet.

High-energy breeds (Border Collies, Australian Shepherds, Huskies, working breeds) benefit from a mix of physical exercise and mental work like scent games, training sessions, or puzzle feeders. Pure physical exercise alone often isn't enough.
Crates provide a safe den-like space for dogs. Adult dogs should not be crated longer than 4 to 6 hours at a time. Never use the crate as punishment.
Pets need constant access to fresh water. Dogs need about 1 ounce of water per pound of body weight per day. Cats often prefer moving water from a fountain, which encourages higher intake.
